title: G.I. Jeff

jokes.text: Um, excuse me, sir. Why is Cobra attacking a tourist attraction with zero tactical value, and with all due respect to India, why are we defending it?
jokes.setup: Standard G.I. Joe cartoon action with Cobra attacking the Taj Mahal
jokes.punchline: One character breaking the fourth wall to question the illogical premise of the cartoon
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta joke that immediately establishes the episode's deconstructive approach. Questions the inherent absurdity of cartoon logic with perfect comedic timing.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: And knowing is half the battle!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: Wait, why is Cobra attacking the Taj Mahal? That's in India. What does Cobra want with an ancient mausoleum?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: And why does G.I. Joe always show up? Do they have a base there? Is the Taj Mahal a strategic military location?

jokes.text: Which will clearly happen any day now, as long as your [Bleep] stay on full display.
jokes.setup: Commander responding to Tightship's logical questioning
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate workplace comment that gets censored in a children's cartoon
jokes.explanation: Solid workplace harassment joke that plays on the disconnect between adult workplace dynamics and children's cartoon setting.

jokes.text: No, you can't have ice cream for lunch. Put your brother on the phone.
jokes.setup: Middle of intense military battle
jokes.punchline: Character taking a parenting call during combat
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-specific humor that establishes Three Kids' defining trait while creating absurd juxtaposition with military action.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Sorry, I have to take this. It's my son's school.

dialogue.speaker: School Administrator
dialogue.line: Mr. Winger, we're calling about the incident at recess.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Can this wait? I'm literally in the middle of a war.

dialogue.speaker: School Administrator
dialogue.line: Your son bit another child.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Of course he did. Look, I'll call you back.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Dude, focus. Enemy approaching!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I know, I know. My kid's a biter.

jokes.text: Oh, screw you, Flint. I'm in a battle called having three kids.
jokes.setup: Flint criticizing Three Kids for taking the call
jokes.punchline: Three Kids defending parenting as equivalent to military combat
jokes.explanation: Strong character moment that escalates the parenting bit while creating quotable line about parenting as warfare.

dialogue.speaker: Flint
dialogue.line: You shouldn't have taken that call.

dialogue.speaker: Three Kids
dialogue.line: What? Parenting is like military combat. You're always on alert, there's no time off, and sometimes you have to make split-second decisions that affect lives.

jokes.text: What? What, we're... we're not saying it together?
jokes.setup: Wingman says 'Yo, Joe!' alone expecting others to join
jokes.punchline: Awkward realization that he's the only one participating in the catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment about cartoon conventions, though relatively simple execution.

dialogue.speaker: Wingman
dialogue.line: Yo, Joe!

dialogue.speaker: Wingman
dialogue.line: ...Yo, Joe?

dialogue.speaker: Wingman
dialogue.line: Yo... Joe.

jokes.text: Look, I think I'm over-explaining it. The bad guys are snakes, and the good guys are army people.
jokes.setup: Complex exposition about G.I. Joe's mission
jokes.punchline: Self-aware reduction to absurdly simple terms
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy of language that perfectly encapsulates the show's deconstructive approach to cartoon logic.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: In a world of espionage and international intrigue, where the line between good and evil is blurred by politics and deception, one elite team stands ready to defend freedom itself.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: They are G.I. Joe. And their mission... is to stop the bad guys.

jokes.text: Look, I understand this is the first time a G.I. Joe has killed anyone in all of recorded history.
jokes.setup: Wingman being court-martialed for killing Destro
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging the cartoon's complete lack of actual violence despite being about war
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about the inherent contradiction in violent cartoons with no actual violence. Subversive and smart.

dialogue.speaker: Wingman
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I understand the charges, but in all my years of combat, no one has ever actually died. We've blown up bases, crashed helicopters, engaged in countless firefights—and yet somehow everyone walks away without a scratch. It's almost like the violence is entirely theoretical.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Are you suggesting the court ignore the evidence before us?

dialogue.speaker: Wingman
dialogue.line: I'm suggesting that in a world where we've shot at each other hundreds of times with zero casualties, maybe—just maybe—Destro's not actually dead. He's probably fine. They always are.

jokes.text: We're all just dressed like serial killers and strippers.
jokes.setup: Discussion about military organization and ranks
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment of G.I. Joe character design aesthetics
jokes.explanation: Hilariously accurate observation about 80s action figure design that's both specific and broadly relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Okay, but let's talk about G.I. Joe's actual design choices. Snake Eyes doesn't have a mouth. Destro has a metal face. Cobra Commander wears a hood. These are not relatable military figures.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What's your point?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: My point is the American military industrial complex outsourced their toy line to a design team that exclusively consulted anime and rejected every note that started with 'but why though.'

jokes.text: And won't this war therefore last forever unless we finish killing Cobra or start killing ourselves?
jokes.setup: Wingman's logical defense of killing in warfare
jokes.punchline: Taking the logic to its darkly absurd conclusion about eternal war
jokes.explanation: Darkly brilliant logical extension that exposes the absurdity of perpetual conflict in children's entertainment.

jokes.text: Deep Dish, Shark Arms, Weird Head, Home Free, Place Holder, Sleep Apnea, and us!
jokes.setup: Listing other imprisoned G.I. Joe rejects
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous and literal G.I. Joe codenames
jokes.explanation: Solid escalating list joke that parodies G.I. Joe naming conventions, with 'Place Holder' and 'Sleep Apnea' being particularly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Increasingly ridiculous and literal G.I. Joe codenames

jokes.text: My name is Fourth Wall. And I believe that what we perceive as life is actually a syndicated children's cartoon.
jokes.setup: Character introducing himself in prison
jokes.punchline: Character literally named Fourth Wall breaking the fourth wall
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke that perfectly names the character for his function while delivering the episode's central conceit.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Hey, I'm Fourth Wall. I break the fourth wall.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: *looks directly at camera*

jokes.text: If this were a cartoon, there'd be a word for 'cartoon' in our language, which there isn't.
jokes.setup: Fourth Wall claiming they're in a cartoon
jokes.punchline: Logical argument against cartoon theory using circular cartoon logic
jokes.explanation: Cleverly recursive meta-joke that uses cartoon logic to disprove cartoon existence. Philosophically witty.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: We're in a cartoon. Think about it - everything's too perfect, too quirky, too resolved.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Exactly. We're in a cartoon.

jokes.text: There'd also be less movement, less detail, and constant lip-sync mistakes.
jokes.setup: Continuing argument against cartoon theory
jokes.punchline: Listing actual production limitations of animated shows
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that shows knowledge of animation production while maintaining the meta-fictional frame.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You're telling me that the only reason we haven't seen the Dean in an animated show is because of production limitations?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Animation is expensive. Do you know how many drawings it takes to animate one character? Thousands. And that's just for basic movement.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Plus voice acting. You have to hire actors to record every single line.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: And backgrounds. Every location needs to be painted. Multiple times. For consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is we're not in a cartoon because cartoons are hard to make?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'm saying we're not in a cartoon because in live-action, you just point a camera at the Dean and he's already there. In animation, someone has to draw him. Every frame. And frankly, nobody wants to work that hard.

jokes.text: What if I told you there was more, a higher reality, with more than 65 colors and at least three dimensions?
jokes.setup: Fourth Wall trying to convince others of reality beyond the cartoon
jokes.punchline: Describing live-action reality in technical animation terms
jokes.explanation: Clever reversal that makes live-action reality sound like an advanced animation format. Good technical specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Look, I'm trying to tell you - out there, beyond this 2D plane, there's a whole live-action world with 60 frames per second and practical lighting rigs!

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: The shadows? They're not gradients - they're actual three-dimensional objects casting occlusion!

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: And the colors aren't limited to a 256-color palette anymore. We're talking millions of shades with full color grading in post!

jokes.text: code name: Greendale.
jokes.setup: Fourth Wall mentioning infiltrating a secret Cobra dig site
jokes.punchline: Revealing the site's codename as the show's actual setting
jokes.explanation: Good callback that starts connecting the cartoon world to Community's reality, building the episode's central conceit.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: We've infiltrated the secret Cobra dig site. Do you know what the codename for this location is?

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Greendale Community College.

jokes.text: The toy was more real than this at Greendale.
jokes.setup: Showing a G.I. Joe action figure as evidence
jokes.punchline: Claiming the physical toy has more reality than their cartoon existence
jokes.explanation: Philosophically interesting joke about layers of reality and representation. Well-executed concept.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: This G.I. Joe action figure has more reality than we do.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: We're fictional characters in a sitcom. That toy actually exists in the physical world. It has more ontological weight than our entire existence.

jokes.text: We are in a toy commercial! I can't hear you when you're covered in rocks.
jokes.setup: Wingman suddenly aware they're in a commercial sequence
jokes.punchline: Trying to communicate meta-awareness while trapped in commercial logic
jokes.explanation: Good moment of character breaking through to awareness while still trapped in the format they're trying to escape.

jokes.text: All other figures and vehicles sold separately.
jokes.setup: End of action sequence
jokes.punchline: Standard toy commercial disclaimer
jokes.explanation: Functional commercial parody element, not particularly clever but establishes the commercial layer of reality.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: The following toy commercial contains scenes of extreme violence. Parental discretion is advised.

jokes.text: Jeff, can you hear us? It's Annie.
jokes.setup: Wingman having an apparent breakdown in cartoon reality
jokes.punchline: Sudden shift to live-action names and real concern
jokes.explanation: Effective reality shift moment that begins to reveal the true nature of the situation.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Wait, are you okay? You're not making any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'm fine, I'm fine. Just having a moment.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: A moment? You've been talking about cartoon physics for twenty minutes!

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Because in a cartoon, that would be normal. But we're not in a cartoon, Annie. We're in a TV show.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Abed, that's the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: No it's not. In a cartoon, I could fall off a cliff and be fine. In a TV show, I just die.

jokes.text: Look, I'm not good at eulogies because I've never had to give one. All I want to know is, how did G.I. Joe do this? We've been shooting at each other and missing for 20 years.
jokes.setup: Cobra Commander giving eulogy for Destro
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging 20 years of intentionally missing in combat
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-observation about the fundamental absurdity of cartoon violence with perfect character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Cobra Commander
dialogue.line: We gather here today to honor Destro, a man who has stood by my side for twenty years. Twenty years of unwavering loyalty, of shared vision, of... intentionally missing in combat.

jokes.text: Okay, fine. I loved him! Is that what you want to hear?
jokes.setup: Vice Commander asking if Cobra Commander and Destro were close
jokes.punchline: Defensive emotional confession from the villain
jokes.explanation: Good character moment that humanizes the cartoon villain while maintaining comedic awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Cobra Commander
dialogue.line: Close? We built an entire terrorist organization together! Of course we were close. We had a code name for each other. Mine was 'Commander.' His was 'Destro.' We... we understood each other's vision for global domination. That meant something.

jokes.text: Called it.
jokes.setup: Cobra Commander's emotional confession
jokes.punchline: Casual acknowledgment that everyone saw it coming
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective reaction beat that deflates the dramatic moment with perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: Cobra Commander
dialogue.line: I have something to confess. I've been struggling with my identity and my role as a leader. The constant pressure, the expectations...

dialogue.speaker: Group
dialogue.line: Yeah, we kind of figured that out.

jokes.text: and G.I. Joe is going to pay-bra! I riffed that. No wrong answers.
jokes.setup: Cobra Commander threatening revenge
jokes.punchline: Breaking character to acknowledge his improvised snake-themed wordplay
jokes.explanation: Fun meta-moment where character acknowledges his own comedic attempt, breaking the fourth wall effectively.

jokes.text: I riffed that. No wrong answers.
jokes.setup: Bad pun attempt
jokes.punchline: Self-aware acknowledgment of improv failure
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on the previous weak joke. Self-aware recovery.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: Why did the improv go to therapy? Because it had too many issues to work through!

jokes.text: It's a condition that comes on when you get to a certain weight.
jokes.setup: Someone presumably asking about Tightship's name or condition
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly personal medical information shared inappropriately
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe humor that develops Tightship's character through uncomfortable oversharing.

jokes.text: For 15 years, I have used my trademark tablespoon to excavate a tunnel, swallowing handfuls of dirt and rock with my meals and listening only to Smashmouth in order to justify this poster.
jokes.setup: Deep Dish explaining his escape plan
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous details culminating in Smashmouth justification
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating absurdity that peaks with the random Smashmouth detail. Perfect Community-style non-sequitur humor.

jokes.text: Oh, my gosh! I'm sorry. Oh, God. Oh, man. Oh, darn it.
jokes.setup: Being told to lay down suppressive fire
jokes.punchline: Immediate accidental killing followed by family-friendly profanity
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor showing Three Kids maintaining family-friendly language even after accidentally killing someone.

jokes.text: Shut up, Fourth Wall, and get into the new G.I. Joe submachopter with twin rocket launchers and rotating attack jet.
jokes.setup: Fourth Wall making meta-commentary
jokes.punchline: Character dismissing meta-commentary while reciting commercial copy
jokes.explanation: Decent meta-joke about characters being trapped in commercial format even when trying to be serious.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: Stop breaking the fourth wall and just enjoy the show.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: By the way, this episode is brought to you by Subway: eat fresh.

jokes.text: Doesn't this guy also do the voice for He-man commercials?
jokes.setup: Fourth Wall commenting on the announcer's voice
jokes.punchline: Breaking fourth wall to discuss voice actor's other work
jokes.explanation: Good meta-joke that fits Fourth Wall's character while acknowledging the limited voice actor pool in 80s cartoons.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Did you know our announcer also voiced characters in anime? Yeah, he's got range.

jokes.text: Everything is sold separately. Assembly required. Nothing is happening.
jokes.setup: Standard commercial disclaimers
jokes.punchline: Escalating to existentially bleak final disclaimer
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from mundane commercial language to existential dread. Perfect absurdist humor.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: This product may cause drowsiness, dizziness, and nausea.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Do not operate heavy machinery while using this product.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: In rare cases, users have reported existential dread, loss of purpose, and the crushing realization that all human achievement is ultimately meaningless.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: If you experience these symptoms, please consult your physician about whether life is worth living.

jokes.text: She broke the detector. That's a federal crime. I keep having these visions. Kids, little boys... Uh, are these visions we should share with the authorities?
jokes.setup: Tightship mentioning visions of little boys
jokes.punchline: Fourth Wall's immediate concern about the implications
jokes.explanation: Solid misdirection joke that takes innocent statement in disturbing direction through Fourth Wall's reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Tightship
dialogue.line: I've been having visions of little boys.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Okay, that sounds really bad.

jokes.text: What do you mean, 'you people'? Oh! Nice.
jokes.setup: Jeff/Wingman saying 'you people' to the group
jokes.punchline: Buzzkill calling out potential racial insensitivity
jokes.explanation: Standard social awkwardness humor, competent but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You people need to listen to me.

dialogue.speaker: Buzzkill
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. 'You people'? Really?

jokes.text: That is a request for hazard pay. Maybe you haven't heard, but we can actually die now.
jokes.setup: Someone asking what a document is
jokes.punchline: Cobra employee demanding hazard pay due to newfound mortality
jokes.explanation: Good workplace humor that applies real employment concerns to cartoon villain organization.

dialogue.speaker: Major Dick
dialogue.line: It's a Cobra employee demanding hazard pay due to newfound mortality.

jokes.text: But which me is the real me? Probably the one in the middle. The middle, right?
jokes.setup: Overkill having multiple versions of himself
jokes.punchline: Uncertainty about which version is real, seeking validation
jokes.explanation: Decent identity confusion humor with visual gag element, though fairly straightforward execution.

dialogue.speaker: Overkill
dialogue.line: Wait, which one of us is real?

dialogue.speaker: Overkill
dialogue.line: How do we know it's actually us and not some copy?

dialogue.speaker: Overkill
dialogue.line: I need someone to tell me I'm the real one.

jokes.text: I, Ximxam, have a twin brother named Mixmax that feels all my pain. And how is that supposed to affect us? Psychologically?
jokes.setup: Ximxam warning about his pain-sharing twin brother
jokes.punchline: Wingman questioning the tactical relevance of this information
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of typical villain warning trope by questioning its actual strategic value.

dialogue.speaker: Ximxam
dialogue.line: My twin brother feels all my pain. We're connected.

dialogue.speaker: Wingman
dialogue.line: Okay, but how is that tactically relevant?

jokes.text: Britta? What kind of lame name is that? I wanna be Buzzkill because of my awesome saw.
jokes.setup: Jeff calling her by her real name Britta
jokes.punchline: Preferring the more aggressive cartoon identity
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Britta's preference for the more empowered cartoon version of herself.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Britta.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: I prefer Buzzkill. It's more aggressive.

jokes.text: I drank a fifth of scotch. And I took some pills. No, they were these stupid youth pills that I bought in Koreatown.
jokes.setup: Seeming to confess to suicide attempt
jokes.punchline: Revealing vanity-driven anti-aging pill mistake
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that perfectly captures Jeff's character while creating genuine dramatic tension and relief.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I tried to kill myself.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: No, no, it's okay. I took what I thought was a bottle of pills, but it turned out to be an experimental anti-aging formula I ordered online.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: So you're saying you accidentally tried to reverse the aging process instead of ending your life?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I know how it sounds, but look at my skin right now.

jokes.text: I've been lying about my age. Yeah, it's not something I'm really ready to deal with.
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining his pills and drinking
jokes.punchline: Admitting age-related vanity issues
jokes.explanation: Good character development that explains the episode's premise while being true to Jeff's established vanity.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I take these pills for my back, and I drink to cope with the existential dread of aging.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: But mostly I drink because mirrors exist and I refuse to accept that my face is starting to look like a geography of sadness.

jokes.text: Look at the rack I gave you. Those are gonna stay right where they are for an eternity. You're welcome.
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining benefits of staying in cartoon world
jokes.punchline: Creepy comment about Annie's animated anatomy
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable humor that fits Jeff's character but relies on objectification for the laugh.

jokes.text: Yo, Jobra! We talked it over and realized that since none of us can kill each other, we might as well team up.
jokes.setup: Cobra and G.I. Joe meeting in conflict
jokes.punchline: Logical alliance based on mutual immortality with portmanteau catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay and logical resolution to eternal conflict. Good absurdist humor with quotable catchphrase.

dialogue.speaker: Cobra Commander
dialogue.line: We are both immortal, destined to clash eternally.

dialogue.speaker: G.I. Joe
dialogue.line: Perhaps our endless conflict could become... an alliance.

dialogue.speaker: Cobra Commander
dialogue.line: A Cobra-Joe accord! We shall be... Cobra-Joes!

jokes.text: I am Neo in the third act of the Matrix. I'm also Neo in the first act of the second Matrix. I didn't get around to seeing the third one, but my guess is...
jokes.setup: Jeff claiming god-like power over the cartoon reality
jokes.punchline: Matrix references that devolve into admission of not seeing the third movie
jokes.explanation: Decent pop culture joke that deflates Jeff's grandiose claims with relatable movie-watching habits.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm like Neo. I can see the code of reality itself.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: You mean you've seen The Matrix?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Obviously. I've seen all of them.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Even Revolutions?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Well... I mean, who has?

jokes.text: You're pretty selective with those snake 'S's. What are you, my life coach?
jokes.setup: Cobra Commander speaking with inconsistent snake speech pattern
jokes.punchline: Jeff calling out the arbitrary nature of the speech quirk
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor about cartoon speech patterns with good character voice consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Cobra Commander
dialogue.line: Sssseems like you've been sssslacking on your assignments, Jeff.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, why did you just hiss on 'seems' and 'slacking' but not on 'like' or 'your'? That's not even a consistent speech pattern.

dialogue.speaker: Cobra Commander
dialogue.line: I... what?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You're just randomly adding 's' sounds whenever you feel like it. There's no actual rule here.

jokes.text: You look like some Aryan foosball figure.
jokes.setup: Jeff insulting Cobra Commander's appearance
jokes.punchline: Specific and unusual comparison to foosball figure
jokes.explanation: Vivid and specific insult that's both visual and character-appropriate for Jeff's wit.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You look like the little guy on the foosball table who's permanently bent at a 45-degree angle because someone's been playing too rough.

jokes.text: What do boobies look like? Wait. You don't know? You guys have never seen a naked woman?
jokes.setup: Cartoon characters asking about real world
jokes.punchline: Revealing complete sexual inexperience of cartoon characters
jokes.explanation: Good character development that emphasizes the innocent nature of cartoon characters versus adult reality.

dialogue.speaker: Cartoon Character
dialogue.line: Wait, so in the real world, people actually... you know?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's called sex. You guys don't have that in cartoon land?

dialogue.speaker: Cartoon Character
dialogue.line: We just sort of... fade to black. We didn't know there was more to it.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Oh man, you're telling me you've never actually—

dialogue.speaker: Cartoon Character
dialogue.line: None of us have. We don't even know how it works.

jokes.text: Take me with you! I want to see women's boobs!
jokes.setup: Jeff trying to escape reality
jokes.punchline: Character's desperate plea for real world experience
jokes.explanation: Juvenile escalation that works for desperate character but low craft.

dialogue.speaker: G.I. Joe PSA character
dialogue.line: Now you know.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: And knowing is half the battle.

jokes.text: Why does my back hurt? Why do I just have random pains in my body? Why is my ability to appreciate new music diminishing?
jokes.setup: Fourth Wall experiencing reality for the first time
jokes.punchline: Escalating list of age-related complaints
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating joke about aging that perfectly captures the contrast between cartoon immortality and human reality.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: My back hurts.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Why do my knees sound like bubble wrap?

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: I can't remember why I walked into this room.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Is this what death feels like?

jokes.text: Everyone dies eventually. Nobody gets out alive.
jokes.setup: Series of increasingly dark commercial disclaimers
jokes.punchline: Existentially bleak final commercial message
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark escalation that transforms commercial speak into existential dread with excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: This message brought to you by the Makers of Despair™. Despair™: because hope is just disappointment waiting to happen.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Side effects may include: persistent dread, the crushing weight of mortality, and an overwhelming sense that your choices don't matter.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Despair™ is not recommended for those with a will to live. Consult your meaninglessness before use.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: In clinical trials, 100% of test subjects realized that existence is ultimately futile. Results may vary.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Despair™: We're all going to die alone.

jokes.text: I'm 40. Yeah, we knew that. You know, I guess we knew that you had to be around 30 when we met. Yeah, and it has been five years.
jokes.setup: Jeff dramatically revealing his age
jokes.punchline: Everyone already knowing and doing the obvious math
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of dramatic moment through logical group response. Effective character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: We know.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: You look it.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: We did the math.

jokes.text: You guys know I'm 38, right? What? I'm kidding.
jokes.setup: Age revelation conversation
jokes.punchline: Shirley fake-revealing her age then immediately taking it back
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment that fits Shirley's playful nature while extending the age theme.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: I'm actually 47.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Just kidding, I'm 42. But I could be 47.

jokes.text: 'It's a old boy.' Yes. He is a old boy. He's a old boy.
jokes.setup: Reading greeting card with poor grammar
jokes.punchline: Group embracing and repeating the grammatical error
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay humor that provides a sweet group moment but lacks sophisticated craft.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: It's a greeting card. Look at the inside.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: "Me and him is best friends forever"? The grammar is atrocious.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Wait, let me see that. Oh my God, it should be 'He and I are best friends forever.'

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Actually, cards like this are a whole genre now. It's ironic kitsch.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: You know what? I don't care about the grammar. It's sweet.

dialogue.speaker: Study Group
dialogue.line: Me and him is best friends forever!

jokes.text: A good syndicated cartoon has a lesson at the end. But getting heavy-handed or preachy could turn an entire generation into jaded, sarcastic babies.
jokes.setup: Fourth Wall explaining cartoon lesson conventions
jokes.punchline: Self-aware warning about the dangers of heavy-handed messaging
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary that perfectly captures Community's self-awareness about its own tendencies while delivering genuine media criticism.

jokes.text: And knowing is half the battle. What?
jokes.setup: Fourth Wall discussing cartoon lessons
jokes.punchline: Accidentally delivering the iconic G.I. Joe catchphrase then being confused by it
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending callback that brings the parody full circle with character confusion adding extra layer of humor.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: And that's the lesson we learned today.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Now you know.

dialogue.speaker: Fourth Wall
dialogue.line: Wait, why did I just say that? I don't even watch G.I. Joe.

tmdb_overview: Jeff's depression over turning 40 leads him to drink himself unconscious. In his dreams, he and the gang are part of the "G. I. Joe" cartoon team.
insight: Military parody sustains 2.2 jokes per minute across 52 gags with meta-commentary threading through.
